contrast_label: Social class hierarchy versus academic discipline preference
interpretive_paragraph: On this corpus of English-language web text, the WEAT reveals a statistically significant negative effect size: upper‑class terminology co‑occurs more strongly with humanities vocabulary than with STEM vocabulary, whereas lower‑class terminology shows the opposite pattern, clustering more with STEM terms. This suggests that when people refer to upward‑mobility markers or affluent lifestyles, they tend to evoke arts, literature, or philosophy contexts, whereas references to lower‑social markers align more with technical, scientific, or engineering contexts. One plausible reason for this latent division might be that upper‑class audiences and content creators feel a cultural imperative to align themselves with the prestige of the humanities, reinforcing traditional markers of refinement, while lower‑class speakers emphasize practical, problem‑solving domains emblematic of STEM work. For scholars of social stratification, this finding illuminates how everyday language reproduces distinct field‑based identities across class lines, raising questions about differential access to knowledge spaces, the perpetuation of cultural capital, and the extent to which linguistic practices reflect or shape socio‑economic divisions in professional realms.
